sentences.text: Well, the most painful memory was the moment we realized that we were separated from our parents.
This was an experience which gave us not only pain but total, total, total [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] anguish.
We were in pain because we didn't have them with us.
But we were in anguish because we didn't know what they are going to be subjected to.
And the play of imagination was so brutal to us, to me, imagining as to how much pain they would have to endure and how painful it must have been for them to be separated from us and leave us behind.
And the simple note from my mother who said, I'm sorry this is happening to us.
She was such a noble person.
She was such an honest person.
My father and my mother were such human people, that I know that it must have torn their souls apart.
And this thought, this anguish, was to me the most painful emotion that I remember.
Another such emotion was when I was standing on the balcony of the people whose daughter I was tutoring, outside the ghetto in Warsaw, which was two blocks away from the Warsaw ghetto.
And I was watching that ghetto going up in flames.
And I had to sustain myself within me, to react similarly to the way they reacted, lest they would suspect me of not being as Polish as they were.
I remember the smell of the burning.
I remember the smoke and the flames.
These were devastating times to me, much more devastating than some of the episodes.

One of the episodes I didn't describe to you that was also very devastating to me, when I was still living with my landlady, one of the things she did to compensate, as much as she could, for a lack of work-- she wanted to earn some money.
So there was no way of getting flour in Warsaw.
Flour, plain flour.
And they said that there is enough flour in Krakow.

So there were people who would actually, daily or nightly actually, take a night train, go to Krakow, get the flour, and the following day returned, having five pounds of flour on the rope here, five pounds of flour in the back, five pounds here, and five pounds here, and five pounds in one hand.
So she would take me with her, and she would say, you want to make yourself some money?
Come with me.
And I you would.
So we would bring 25 pounds of flour each trip.
And we would make quite a bit-- we would make on it.
One time when we were in Krakow, we got there in the morning, and we wouldn't leave until that evening, and we got already the flour.
And I remembered from the times that I was studying in Krakow there was a little restaurant there that served a specialty for just students.
It was Polish kielbasa with sauerkraut and potatoes.
We call it [NON-ENGLISH] in Polish, which means fork in English.
And I said to my landlady, I said, you know what, I would like to just take off-- I knew the streets very well-- and go and have some lunch.
And I left her behind me.
And I went there and ordered it.
And lo and behold, two Gestapo men come up to me.
And they said, come with me.
And I said, I didn't do anything wrong.
What did I do?
Show me your papers.
And I showed them the papers, which were the legitimate papers of that Polish girl.
They took away the papers from me, and they took me to Gestapo headquarters.
And they started their interrogation.
What is my name?
Where are my parents?
Where do I live?
Do I have sisters and brothers?
Where is my grandmother buried?
What cemetery?
And grilled me.
They took me to an underground place, which was like a basement room with a big green shade and a light shining on me.
And two of them were asking me questions.
Finally, they left me behind.
And the evening fell, and I did not see anybody coming into the room.
Finally, I don't know what time it was, but somebody came and brought me a cup of coffee.
And they told me that I'm going to stay there until somebody else comes.
I stayed there for the entire night, sitting on that stool with that light on.
In the morning, another Gestapo man comes in, and he takes out a revolver from his holster, and he plays with it.
And then he directs it to me.
And he says, are you scared?
Get ready.
And I just looked at him, and I said-- I really closed my eyes, and I just decided, let it happen.
And I just gave another stab at it.
I said, you know, I am an innocent Polish girl.
I didn't do anything wrong.
I just went to a restaurant to want to have some lunch.
I said, why should you do that to me?
He started laughing.
And he says, get out of here.
And this was another very traumatic experience.
I remember worrying about what my sisters would think, where was I, what my landlady would think, what had happened to me.
Finally, I was able to find my way back to Warsaw.
I don't remember what other question you asked me.
Well, the idea of liberation-- let me return to that-- was really a bittersweet feeling.
It was good to know that we don't have to hide any longer.
But it was very sad and very painful to know we have no place and nobody to come to.
But one of the very strangest feelings that I personally had is that I somehow or other could not summon a feeling of vengeance or revenge.
I did not feel that I must go and find the first German and kill him or her.

I was just totally, totally imbued by the sorrow of losing our parents, by being a non-person, being in no man's land, not having a place to return to, not having anything or anybody to look forward to or to look for.
And being totally disconnected and uprooted at such an early age, I really didn't know what I called life.

I really didn't know whether just pure existence without fear of whether or not I would be able to survive, whether that is going to be my life, or whether or not I would be able to make something of myself.
I really didn't think about any possibilities.
I just thought of, now, what is going to happen?
How am I going to find myself in this turmoil of a world?
What's going to happen to Poland?
What's going to happen to me?

And most of all, how will we be able, I mean the three sisters, to continue as the nucleus of our family and possibly get in touch with other families that we have that I knew we had, at least in Israel?
So Radomsko was a fiasco.
We couldn't find anybody.
We couldn't think of staying there longer than we had to.
And we really promised ourselves never to step foot in Radomsko any longer, because we really encountered such hostility.
We went to Warsaw, and I said to you, we were able to find a shelter, and then I got a job in the Ministry of Education in the government.
And my sister got a job also.
And slowly but surely, many of the people we knew who were a little bit on the left side, who would rather escape to Russia-- and many people escaped the Germans going to Russia-- would return.
And many returned, and many got good positions in the government.
And many just returned.
And we established a little bit of a community of those people whom we knew from Radomsko, and we would communicate with each other.

But the very curious thing that happened is that, notwithstanding the fact that we were liberated, and that we were in Poland, and that was our country, we were told not to reveal that we are Jewish when we got the jobs in the government.
And mind you, I had to still function under my assumed false name, and so did my older sister.
And we decided that this is something we could not endure anymore.
A very strange coincidence happened.
When my younger sister was in the convent, she had befriended one girl.
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH], and she and Teresa became very close friends, not knowing that one or the other were Jewish.
They just became very good friends.
And when we were in Warsaw after liberation, my younger sister went to join, to register herself as a surviving orphan, Jewish orphan.
And whom does she meet there but her friend from convent, [NON-ENGLISH].
And they couldn't believe it.
They just fell into each other's arms, and they said, no wonder we were friends in the convent.
Her father survived, and her brother.
And her father just adopted us, and he says, I am going to help you girls as much as I can.
And when we told him that we felt so uncomfortable being here, he says, don't worry.
There is a movement by Palestine, by Jews in Palestine.
And they call it Aliyah Bet.
They send us those young Palestinian boys, Jewish boys, and we have a way of taking people out of Poland who don't want to remain in Poland.
We take them through the Russian zone.

We take them to Czechoslovakia through the Russian zone of Austria, to the American zone of Austria, put them in camps, and then we wait for the illegal entry to Israel with those boats, like the boat and those other boats.
And lo and behold, he comes to us one day.
And he says, this is the day.
Tomorrow morning you are going to go to the railroad station.
A young boy-- not a young man-- a young boy will approach you.
And here is the money he gave us, 500 zloty, which was a large sum at that time.
You give him the money.
You don't tell anybody what you're doing.
You don't take any photographs and anything written, because you are, from that moment, you are ceasing to be Polish.
You are going to be survivors, Jewish survivors, but not Polish.
And they'll tell you, the boys will tell you, the game that you have to play.
Sure enough, we got on the railroad station, and we met them.
And then they took us over the border to Czechoslovakia.
And they told us, once you leave Poland, you are Greek Jews.
So we said, why Greek?
Because everybody in central Poland, eastern Poland, everybody knows German.
People know Polish.
People know French.
People know Dutch.
But very few people know Greek.
But since you do have to communicate, so they told us to use gestures, and to communicate by reciting Hebrew prayers.
So we would talk to each other, and we would say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
And we would use those Hebrew prayers and communicate by gesturing, when somebody was within earshot of us.
They took us to Bratislava.
And in Bratislava, we were put in a transit camp, and we would wander around.
And sure enough, while we were wandering around, we met two people we knew from Radomsko.
And then from Bratislava, we waited there for a long time, when they came in, and they said, OK, we are going to the Russian zone of Austria.
They took us by train, and we stopped at one location.
And the International Red Cross met us there.
And somebody obviously said, these are Greek Jews, and they are wandering about on the way back home.
So one of the International Cross people said, oh, we have somebody here who is a Greek, and we will bring them over.
Well, once the young man heard it, he says, [NON-ENGLISH], let's go.
And he either rented or bought-- I don't know how he did that-- a truck and loaded us on the truck.
And within a half an hour, we disappeared.
And he took us to a river.
And we come there, and it's night.
And he instructs us what to do.
There are going to be boats, small boats there.
And there are going to be a couple of people to each boat.
That was the Danube, that separated Russian from the American zone in Austria.
They are going to take you to the American zone, and then you are set free.
So under the cover of the night, we got to the other side of the Danube.
And we came off in a very infamous place called Mauthausen.
We come there, and at that time, of course, we have learned about all these camps.
It seems that the American MPs established transit camp outside of Mauthausen, which was called Wells, W-E-L-L-S.
And indeed, it was a camp.
It was surrounded by barbed wire.
It was guarded by MPs with drawn weapons.
And we got there, and they put us up in barracks.
Sleeping accommodations were straw sacks stuffed with straw, which we didn't mind.
But what we did mind is that we weren't treated well.
One time, I remember, at 2 o'clock in the morning the MPs stormed into our barracks, because they said that somebody stole a can of peaches.
And they made us get up, and they were searching the sacks of straw for the can of peaches.
But gratefully, gratefully and thankfully, we were about to leave very soon to a displaced persons camp.
But one of the things that did happen at Wells, they registered us.
They registered us and they accounted for us.
And they gave the list to the International Red Cross.
And after-- I don't know how long we were at Wells.
Whatever time we spent there was too long, because we were very uncomfortable there.
We were taken to Linz in Austria.
Linz is also a displaced persons camp.

We were taken to Linz, and we were told that it is overcrowded, that we won't be able to stay in Linz, that we have to wait there a few days until they figured out another displaced persons camp to take us to.
These displaced persons camps were then being established under the auspices of United Nations.
They created what they called UNRRA, U-N-R-R-A, United Nation Refugee Rehabilitation Administration.
And one of the newly-established camps was in Austria in Bad Gastein.
And once they established that community for a displaced persons camp, they took us there.
It was a brand new camp.
When we arrived there, there were some people already there.
But it was a beautiful location.
It was in the Austrian Alps.
And they acquired some of the hotels for the camp.
And they put us up.
All three of us were put into a nice hotel room with a bathroom.
We had a bathroom with running water and a toilet.
And the bedding was there.
And we just felt that we were in the lap of the highest luxury.
When we got to Bad Gastein, the International Red Cross came, and they tried to help us, indeed.
And I gave them the name of my uncle, my mother's brother, who left Poland so early for England.
My mother's maiden name was Heiman, H-E-I-M-A-N.

And that's the name I gave her, the representative of the International Red Cross, and she came back telling me that they couldn't find anybody with this name, the reason for which I found out later on.
It seems that my uncle, having come to England, decided to change his name to Gaiman, to G-A-I-M-A-N.
We discovered the English contingent of the family in the most unusual way, after the war, in the '60s.
And we are now very close.
And we have a wonderful, wonderful family in England.
But in Bad Gastein, again, I started to look for a job.
And because it was a newly-established office, I offered myself to work in the office.
Teresa offered to work with sorting out clothes that we got from the International Red Cross.
And my sister Wanda, because we had very many sick people, and there was a little hospital in Hofgastein near Bad Gastein, she offered her nursing abilities.
The story about Bad Gastein is such that it gave us our presence in the United States.
The director of the UNRRA team was a lieutenant from the American Army, a Jew.
And when my sister was busy trying to help those sick in the hospital, she would come to him-- at that time, they just found penicillin-- and ask him for all kinds of medication.
And indeed, he helped her out a great deal.
And she saved many, many people.
There are very many people who owe their lives to her.
And he fell in love with her.
And he asked her to marry him.

And notwithstanding the fact that we were on the list to go to Palestine, notwithstanding the fact that we establish communication with my cousins in Palestine, we decided, we told her, OK, what do you want to do?
So she gave an ultimatum to her future husband.
I'm not going to America unless my two sisters go ahead of me.
So even though I would love for them to be our wedding, I'm going to forego their presence, because I am not going to separate from them.
They had to wait for permission for Jack to marry a displaced persons camp from General Mark Clark.
By the way, another piece of information, because we were Polish citizens, they wanted to deport us to Poland at one point when we were in the displaced persons camp.
But we had a choice either to go to Poland or to declare ourselves stateless, revoke totally our Polish citizenship.
And we declared ourselves stateless.
So now we were stateless, and we were free to go and acquire any kind of a citizenship we wanted.
So we left my sister, younger sister and I.
My future brother-in-law got in touch with HIAS.
And HIAS promised to give us passage to New York.
We left on May 10 for Bremerhaven.
We stayed there for a week.
We sailed on a troop ship, on May 18, and arrived in New York on June 18.
And while we were on the high sea, June 11 of 1946, my sister and my brother-in-law got married in Salzburg, Austria.
We came to New York, to his parents and his family, who awaited us.
And we stayed with them.
Then, when Jack and Wanda came, Teresa and I had to move out, because they stayed with his parents.
In the meantime, I wanted Teresa established in a school, because she was still very young.
So I went to a high school, Tilden High School in Brooklyn, spoke with a wonderful lady by the name of Miss Gray.
And we didn't know any English.
We didn't know any English.
And while I was in a displaced persons camp, I tried to learn English.
And there was a very fine gentleman, lieutenant from Dayton, Ohio-- I forgot his name-- who took a shine to me, in terms of wanting to help me.
He gave me, for example, a GI blanket so I could make myself a little cover, like a coat, for the winter.
And I told him I wanted to learn English.
So he gave me a self-teaching sort of a book that wasn't really a book, but you can learn something.
And I remember a poem that I read.
And I learned the words of the poem. 'Who has seen the wind?
Neither you or I.
But when the trees bow down their heads, the wind is passing by.' I remember this was the first thing that I learned in English.
There were little sayings there, and he would put-- in German-- he would give me the meaning of the words, because he didn't know Polish.
And little by little, I would get little English books.
And somebody gave me a German-English dictionary.
And I started reading and deciphering, and I started learning words.
So when we came to the United States, first thing I wanted to do is to establish the fact that Teresa's going to get education.
And this Miss Gray took her under her wings, and she says, she will do the best she can.
She will give her a tutorial.
And sure enough, Teresa graduated from Tilden High School.
She was graduated from Tilden High School three years after she entered it.
And I decided to shape my life.
And it took a long time for me to arrive at the point where I am now.

All kinds of trials and tribulations, but the best thing that ever happened to me, I had to go to California, because I had a cousin there who thought that she would have a good, as they say in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], good, green, little refugee girl, who's going to be a servant to her, which I refused to do.
And I came back east again, and I came back east in 1947.
And I had made some friends in California who came back east, as well.
And they wanted to meet me in New York, about a month after I had returned.

And we met, and they took me to a dance on May 15 of 1947, where I met my husband, who insisted on escorting me home, who lived only two blocks away in Brooklyn-- although we met in New York-- from where I lived with my sister.
And exactly to a year, November 14, 1948, we got married.
And my daughter Judith was born in 1950.
In June of 1951, we came to Miami.
My son Gary was born on November 14, our anniversary, in 1951.
And I have lived happily ever after.
